one_line_takeaway: By replacing global loss-averaging with sign-based sliding-window voting across geometrically spaced window sizes, WBC substantially improves membership inference against fine-tuned LLMs (AUC ~0.826-0.839 average vs ~0.754 for the strongest prior baseline, with 2-3x gains in true-positive rate at low false-positive-rate thresholds), and the attack survives DP-SGD and LoRA to a meaningful degree but is largely neutralized by selective data-paraphrasing defenses (SOFT).
key_finding: On the six Cosmopedia datasets in Table 1, WBC achieves the best AUC and TPR-at-low-FPR on every dataset and metric, e.g. AUC 0.837 vs Ratio's 0.703 on Khan Academy, 0.854 vs 0.781 on Stanford, 0.843 vs 0.788 on Web Samples v2, 0.814 vs 0.768 on AutoMathText, 0.802 vs 0.714 on WikiHow. Averaged across the reported datasets WBC reaches AUC 0.826 vs the strongest baseline Ratio's 0.754 (abstract/intro report an overall 0.839 vs 0.754 across all 11 datasets, with TPR@1%FPR improving 2.8x, from 5.2% to 14.6%). At Khan Academy's 0.1% FPR, WBC detects 2.6% of members vs 0.7% for the next-best baseline (Ensemble), a 3.7x gain; on Web Samples v2, TPR@1%FPR is 19.8% for WBC vs 9.4% for the best baseline (Difference). Reference-free baselines plateau near AUC 0.6, confirming a reference model is necessary. Ablations show the sign-based aggregator (AUC 0.835) beats mean (0.728), median (0.818), and min (unspecified but lower) aggregation on Khan Academy; the geometric ensemble (w_min=2, w_max=40, |W|=10) is the best configuration in Table 2 (uniform-spacing ensembles or restricted-range ensembles drop to as low as 0.7836 AUC). Under defenses, WBC degrades gracefully but retains an edge over Ratio: at DP-SGD ε=8, AUC 0.751 vs 0.642 (3.25x TPR@1%FPR advantage); under LoRA rank 32, 2.67x TPR@1%FPR advantage vs 3.95x for full fine-tuning; but the SOFT selective-paraphrasing defense collapses both WBC and Ratio to near-random (AUC ~0.494), the one defense in the paper that neutralizes WBC's advantage.
core_contribution: The paper introduces WBC (Window-Based Comparison), a reference-based membership inference attack (MIA) against fine-tuned LLMs that replaces global loss-averaging with sliding-window, sign-based aggregation of per-token loss differences between a target model M_T and a reference (pre-fine-tuning) model M_R. Motivated by an empirical analysis of over 10 million token-level loss-difference comparisons (Pythia-2.8B fine-tuned on the Khan Academy subset of Cosmopedia), the authors show membership signals are sparse, extremal, spatially unclustered events buried in long-tailed noise from domain-specific tokens, rather than a uniform mean shift (member mean loss-difference 0.393 vs non-member 0.331; excess kurtosis >18; skewness 2.82/2.63). They formalize this with a mixture-of-point-processes model, derive via Pitman asymptotic relative efficiency that a sign test (fraction of windows where reference loss exceeds target loss) is more sample-efficient than a mean-based statistic under long-tailed contamination, and combine sign-test scores across a geometric progression of window sizes (w_min=2 to w_max=40, |W| windows) into a final ensemble score S_WBC.
threat_model: Score-based black-box access: the adversary queries a fine-tuned target model M_T and a reference model M_R (assumed to be the pre-trained base model M_T was fine-tuned from) and receives per-token loss (negative log-likelihood) sequences for a candidate text, not just a single sequence-level average loss. The paper argues this is realistic for (1) open-weight adaptation where practitioners fine-tune public HuggingFace models locally, giving attackers weight access for local inference, and (2) API-based inference via serving backends like vLLM that expose prompt_logprobs. The adversary has no access to model parameters, gradients, or hidden states, and does not know ground-truth membership of any sample. Section 5.5 also tests robustness when M_R is a size- or architecture-mismatched (misaligned) model rather than the exact base model.
limitations: The paper's own ablations and defense results identify concrete boundaries: (1) with a mismatched reference model (different architecture, GPT-J-6B vs Pythia-2.8B target), WBC's overall AUC trails Ratio (0.673 vs 0.685), attributed to tokenization/architectural noise degrading local comparisons, though WBC still leads in the low-FPR regime; (2) at very strong differential privacy (ε=1) both WBC and Ratio approach random guessing (AUC 0.561 and 0.524), so the attack is not immune to DP; (3) SOFT (selective paraphrasing of influential samples) reduces WBC from AUC 0.837 to ~0.494, essentially eliminating its advantage entirely; (4) at very small model scale (160M parameters) all methods including WBC perform near-randomly (AUC ~0.51); (5) the geometric window-size ensemble requires empirically tuned w_min/w_max bounds, and the paper's own theory (Section 4.1.4) states the analytically optimal window size cannot be estimated reliably because it depends on unmeasurable per-dataset parameters (signal sparsity, rare-token frequency, signal strength); (6) results rely on the assumption that a suitable reference model (the pre-fine-tuning base model) is available to the attacker, which the authors note is the 'most principled' but not the only tested setting.

quantitative_results: WBC average AUC 0.826 (Table 1, six Cosmopedia datasets) / 0.839 across all 11 datasets (abstract) vs strongest baseline Ratio 0.754
Khan Academy: WBC AUC 0.837 vs Ratio 0.703, TPR@1%FPR 0.146 vs 0.037
Web Samples v2: WBC AUC 0.843, TPR@1%FPR 0.198 vs best baseline Difference 0.094 (2x+)
Khan Academy TPR@0.1%FPR: WBC 2.6% vs Ensemble (next best) 0.7%, 3.7x gain
Sign vs mean aggregation on Khan Academy: AUC 0.835 (sign) vs 0.728 (mean) vs 0.818 (median)
Best ensemble config (w_min=2, w_max=40, |W|=10): highest AUC in Table 2; uniform-spacing alternative drops to 0.8241 AUC (1.5% relative drop)
DP-SGD epsilon=8: WBC AUC 0.751 vs Ratio 0.642; epsilon=1: WBC 0.561 vs Ratio 0.524
LoRA rank 32: WBC TPR@1%FPR 0.048 vs Ratio 0.018 (2.67x), vs 3.95x advantage under full fine-tuning
SOFT defense: WBC AUC drops from 0.837 to ~0.494 (near-random), Ratio drops from 0.703 to ~0.494
Reference model mismatch (GPT-J-6B): WBC AUC 0.673 vs Ratio 0.685 (WBC trails on overall AUC here)
Reference model size mismatch (Pythia-1.4B): WBC AUC 0.774 vs Ratio 0.692
Member vs non-member mean token loss-difference: 0.393 vs 0.331; ~1.77% of member tokens exceed 3 std devs vs 0.3% expected under Gaussian
